case_name: Company Of Management For Baranagar ... vs Atis Dipankar Chowdhuri
facts: Baranagar Jute Factor, a borrower of the State Bank of India, faced winding-up proceedings and a Committee of Management was formed by order of the High Court. The Committee (accused No.1) issued cheque No. 776924 dated 29-8-90 drawn on Union Bank of India; the cheque was presented on 11-9-90 and returned on 12-9-90 with the remark "exceeds arrangement," and again presented on 22-11-90 and returned on 23-11-90 with the remark "refer to drawer." The Bank issued a notice dated 4-12-90 demanding Rs.2.50 lakhs; the Committee replied on 11-12-90 but did not pay, and the Bank filed a complaint under Sections 138/141 Negotiable Instruments Act in the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Calcutta.
arguments_claimant: The petitioners argued (1) prosecution under Section 138 was time-barred/non-compliant because the bank failed to serve the statutory notice within the period specified in the proviso and could not rely on subsequent presentations; (2) the Committee of Management is not a "company" within Section 141 and thus cannot be prosecuted under that provision; and (3) because the Committee was appointed by the High Court it could not be prosecuted without the prior leave/sanction of the Court (analogous to protection afforded to receivers).
arguments_respondent: The opposite party (the Bank) and its counsel relied on authority supporting multiple presentation of a cheque (Sekhar Gupta v. Subhas Chandra Mandal) and contended the prosecution under Section 138/141 was maintainable. It was also argued that the Committee of Management is an association of individuals and could be covered by the definition of a "company" in Section 141, and that any irregularity in not seeking leave of the High Court did not go to the merits of the prosecution and could be remedied by other processes like contempt proceedings.
ratio_decidendi: "There is no prohibition in Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act that a returned cheque cannot be presented again." The court held that the "real cause of action giving rise to a right of prosecution arises from the failure of the drawer of the cheque to make payment to the payee and not from the date of return of the cheque by the bank," and therefore presentation within the periods specified in the proviso is material. The Court also held that where a Committee of Management is constituted by the High Court under a scheme it "should for all practical purposes be deemed to be a representative of the Court and should be treated in the similar way in which a receiver or any other officer appointed by the Court is done," and consequently a prosecution against such a committee without prior leave of the Court is impermissible. Applying these principles the Court concluded that "the prosecution in this case without the prior leave of the Court has been bad" and, although an offence under Section 138 "is found to have been committed," the prosecution nonetheless "fails."
final_order: The criminal revision succeeds; the proceedings in Criminal Case No. C/46/1991 under Sections 138/141 Negotiable Instruments Act pending before the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Calcutta are quashed, and the accused petitioners are discharged and released from their bail bonds.
summary: This is a 1993 Calcutta High Court revisional judgment quashing a prosecution under Sections 138/141 Negotiable Instruments Act. The Court held that Section 138 does not prohibit re-presentation of a dishonoured cheque and that the cause of action arises on failure to pay after notice. Crucially, the Court found that a Committee of Management appointed by the High Court is, for practical purposes, representative of the Court and cannot be prosecuted without prior leave; on that ground the prosecution was quashed.
embedding_summary: Company Of Management For Baranagar vs Atis Dipankar Chowdhuri, Calcutta High Court, 15 February 1993, 1993CRILJ2165. Facts: Baranagar Jute Factor, borrower of State Bank of India, had a Committee of Management appointed by the High Court; cheque No. 776924 dated 29-8-90 drawn on Union Bank was returned (remarks "exceeds arrangement" and later "refer to drawer"); bank issued notice dated 4-12-90 demanding Rs.2.50 lakhs and filed complaint under Sections 138 and 141 Negotiable Instruments Act (Criminal Case C/46/1991) in Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Calcutta. Legal issues: (1) whether Section 138 prohibits re-presentation of a cheque; (2) when the cause of action arises under the proviso (expiry of 15 days after notice); (3) whether a Committee of Management constituted by the High Court is prosecutable under Section 141 or requires prior leave as analogous to a receiver. Outcome: revisional petition allowed, proceedings quashed, accused discharged. Key doctrines: multiple presentation allowed; cause of action arises on failure to pay after notice; prior leave required to prosecute Court-appointed committee.
legal_principles: Section 138 does not prohibit multiple presentation of a returned cheque; "there is no prohibition in Section 138 ... that a returned cheque cannot be presented again."
Cause of action under Section 138 arises on the drawer's failure to make payment after notice under Clause (c) of the proviso, not on the date of the cheque's return.
Court-appointed Committee of Management is for practical purposes representative of the Court and prosecution against it requires prior leave (analogous to protection afforded to receivers).
Penal provisions should be fairly construed to effect legislative intent; strict construction applies only where fair construction is not possible.
A Committee of Management constituted under a court-approved scheme is not to be treated as an ordinary company/association for the purposes of subjecting it to prosecution without sanction.
sections_cited: Sections 78 and 79 Indian Penal Code (referenced in precedent)
cases_cited: Sekhar Gupta v. Subhas Chandra Mandal, 1992 C Cr LR (Cal) 14
Banwarilal Agarwal v. Sudhamoy Basu (Receiver), 59 CWN 481

Sheoratan Agarwal v. State of Madhya Pradesh (referenced)
Kadar Bux Omar Hyat v. Bukt Behari (referenced)

1990 C Cr LR (Cal) 145 (Division Bench decision on retrospective operation of amended Section 138)
bm25_text: Company Of Management For Baranagar ... vs Atis Dipankar Chowdhuri Calcutta High Court 1993 Baranagar Jute Factor, a borrower of the State Bank of India, faced winding-up proceedings and a Committee of Management was formed by order of the High Court. The Committee (accused No.1) issued cheque No. 776924 dated 29-8-90 drawn on Union Bank of India; the cheque was presented on 11-9-90 and returned on 12-9-90 with the remark "exceeds arrangement," and again presented on 22-11-90 and returned on 23-11-90 with the remark "refer to drawer." The Bank issued a notice dated 4-12-90 demanding Rs.2.50 lakhs; the Committee replied on 11-12-90 but did not pay, and the Bank filed a complaint under Sections 138/141 Negotiable Instruments Act in the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Calcutta. The petitioners argued (1) prosecution under Section 138 was time-barred/non-compliant because the bank failed to serve the statutory notice within the period specified in the proviso and could not rely on subsequent presentations; (2) the Committee of Management is not a "company" within Section 141 and thus cannot be prosecuted under that provision; and (3) because the Committee was appointed by the High Court it could not be prosecuted without the prior leave/sanction of the Court (analogous to protection afforded to receivers). The opposite party (the Bank) and its counsel relied on authority supporting multiple presentation of a cheque (Sekhar Gupta v. Subhas Chandra Mandal) and contended the prosecution under Section 138/141 was maintainable. It was also argued that the Committee of Management is an association of individuals and could be covered by the definition of a "company" in Section 141, and that any irregularity in not seeking leave of the High Court did not go to the merits of the prosecution and could be remedied by other processes like contempt proceedings. "There is no prohibition in Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act that a returned cheque cannot be presented again." The court held that the "real cause of action giving rise to a right of prosecution arises from the failure of the drawer of the cheque to make payment to the payee and not from the date of return of the cheque by the bank," and therefore presentation within the periods specified in the proviso is material. The Court also held that where a Committee of Management is constituted by the High Court under a scheme it "should for all practical purposes be deemed to be a representative of the Court and should be treated in the similar way in which a receiver or any other officer appointed by the Court is done," and consequently a prosecution against such a committee without prior leave of the Court is impermissible. Applying these principles the Court concluded that "the prosecution in this case without the prior leave of the Court has been bad" and, although an offence under Section 138 "is found to have been committed," the prosecution nonetheless "fails." The criminal revision succeeds; the proceedings in Criminal Case No. C/46/1991 under Sections 138/141 Negotiable Instruments Act pending before the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Calcutta are quashed, and the accused petitioners are discharged and released from their bail bonds. This is a 1993 Calcutta High Court revisional judgment quashing a prosecution under Sections 138/141 Negotiable Instruments Act. The Court held that Section 138 does not prohibit re-presentation of a dishonoured cheque and that the cause of action arises on failure to pay after notice. Crucially, the Court found that a Committee of Management appointed by the High Court is, for practical purposes, representative of the Court and cannot be prosecuted without prior leave; on that ground the prosecution was quashed. Company Of Management For Baranagar vs Atis Dipankar Chowdhuri, Calcutta High Court, 15 February 1993, 1993CRILJ2165. Facts: Baranagar Jute Factor, borrower of State Bank of India, had a Committee of Management appointed by the High Court; cheque No. 776924 dated 29-8-90 drawn on Union Bank was returned (remarks "exceeds arrangement" and later "refer to drawer"); bank issued notice dated 4-12-90 demanding Rs.2.50 lakhs and filed complaint under Sections 138 and 141 Negotiable Instruments Act (Criminal Case C/46/1991) in Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Calcutta. Legal issues: (1) whether Section 138 prohibits re-presentation of a cheque; (2) when the cause of action arises under the proviso (expiry of 15 days after notice); (3) whether a Committee of Management constituted by the High Court is prosecutable under Section 141 or requires prior leave as analogous to a receiver. Outcome: revisional petition allowed, proceedings quashed, accused discharged. Key doctrines: multiple presentation allowed; cause of action arises on failure to pay after notice; prior leave required to prosecute Court-appointed committee. Section 138 does not prohibit multiple presentation of a returned cheque; "there is no prohibition in Section 138 ... that a returned cheque cannot be presented again." Cause of action under Section 138 arises on the drawer's failure to make payment after notice under Clause (c) of the proviso, not on the date of the cheque's return. Court-appointed Committee of Management is for practical purposes representative of the Court and prosecution against it requires prior leave (analogous to protection afforded to receivers). Penal provisions should be fairly construed to effect legislative intent; strict construction applies only where fair construction is not possible. A Committee of Management constituted under a court-approved scheme is not to be treated as an ordinary company/association for the purposes of subjecting it to prosecution without sanction.
